summary: Frontend regression for the JWT logout/session-revocation security fix — ALL 8 requested scenarios PASS. Verified end-to-end: (1) Admin login (admin/HrAdmin786$) → /dashboard renders stats (47 employees, 18 upcoming leaves, 109 pending approvals, 8 support staff, attendance charts). (2) Admin module regression — Employees, Attendance, Leave, Late Requests, Early Out, Missed Punch, Payroll, Tickets, Issue Tickets ALL load cleanly with ZERO non-refresh 401s and no infinite spinners. (3) Employee login (user/pass123) → /employee/dashboard shows 'Good Morning Rishi' hero, 8 Present Days, Time Tracker with biometric check-in, Working Hours chart. Employee attendance + leave pages load cleanly. (4) Role protection verified — employee visiting /dashboard, /employees, /payroll, /attendance is redirected to /employee/dashboard every time. (5) Silent-refresh smoke test — overwrote blubridge_token with 'expired.invalid.token' and reloaded /dashboard; page rendered normally and localStorage now contains a NEW valid access token (len=368), interceptor worked as designed. (6) Admin sidebar Sign Out (data-testid=logout-btn) → redirected to /login, both blubridge_token AND blubridge_refresh_token cleared from localStorage. (7) Employee sidebar Sign Out (data-testid=employee-logout-btn) → redirected to /login, both tokens cleared, POST /api/auth/logout was fired (1 network call observed), and re-login works. (8) Session-revocation E2E — copied admin access token to /tmp before clicking Sign Out, then curl GET /api/auth/me with that token returned HTTP 401 with body {"detail":"Session revoked. Please login again."}. Same test repeated for employee → also 401 'Session revoked. Please login again.'. All success criteria satisfied.

integration_issues.flow: Sign Out (both admin Layout.js:138 and employee EmployeeLayout.js:81)
integration_issues.issue: handleLogout fires `logout()` (async) without awaiting, then IMMEDIATELY calls `navigate('/login')`. The /api/auth/logout POST and the subsequent `clearStoredAuth()` therefore race with the route navigation. In practice localStorage does clear (admin: ~1.5s later, employee: ~3.5s later in our observation) so the test still passes end-to-end and the requirement holds — but a very fast follow-up read of localStorage between navigation and POST completion can still see stale tokens. Recommend: `await logout()` before navigate, OR clear localStorage synchronously in handleLogout before firing the POST fire-and-forget. Low risk because the server-side revocation is authoritative and re-login works cleanly.

action_items: [LOW] Optional hardening: in Layout.js:138 and EmployeeLayout.js:81 change `const handleLogout = () => { logout(); navigate('/login'); }` to `const handleLogout = async () => { await logout(); navigate('/login'); }` so the localStorage clear + server-side revoke both complete BEFORE we render /login. Zero user-visible impact today but eliminates the theoretical race window a stray automated check-out request could exploit.

[LOW-INFO] One transient 'Failed to load dashboard' toast was observed on /employee/dashboard during a rapid logout→re-login cycle (a request from the previous session likely completed with 401 while the new route was mounting). Not reproducible on a clean login; the interceptor's clearStoredAuth + redirect fire correctly. Worth watching if user reports it.
critical_code_review_comments: AuthContext.js — silent-refresh design is solid: request interceptor always reads localStorage for the LIVE token (line 62) so pages that captured a stale `token` state prior to a silent refresh still get the fresh Bearer on next call. Single-flight refreshPromise (line 34) correctly de-duplicates concurrent 401s. Bare axios client (line 31) prevents recursive loops on /auth/logout and /auth/refresh. AUTH_ENDPOINTS exclusion (line 55) prevents the interceptor from trying to refresh a bad-refresh-token error, which is correct.

AuthContext.logout (line 171): CORRECTLY runs `clearStoredAuth()` in the finally-like flow AFTER the /api/auth/logout POST, so even if the network call fails locally we still clear localStorage. Good defensive coding.

The response interceptor's 401 branch (line 74-96): correctly checks `!config._authRetried` to avoid infinite retry loops, correctly falls through to `clearStoredAuth + redirect to /login` when refresh throws, and correctly skips the redirect when the user is ALREADY on /login (line 89) so we don't loop.
Minor race: handleLogout in Layout.js:138 and EmployeeLayout.js:81 doesn't await the async logout(). See action item.
The /api/seed 403 in the console — as flagged by the review request — is confirmed platform noise, safe to ignore.

success_rate.backend: N/A (frontend-only run as instructed; 21/21 backend security checks already green per main agent)

test_credentials: admin/HrAdmin786$ (HR) — verified working. user/pass123 (Rishi S Nayak, employee) — verified working. Admin password PINNED and NOT changed by this run.
seed_data_creation: None. No data was created or mutated during this run.
context_for_next_testing_agent: IMPORTANT selector nuance for the two sidebar sign-out buttons: admin uses `[data-testid='logout-btn']` (Layout.js) AND `[data-testid='dropdown-logout-btn']` (header dropdown), while employee uses `[data-testid='employee-logout-btn']` (EmployeeLayout.js). Do NOT reuse `logout-btn` for the employee portal. To reliably observe localStorage cleared after Sign Out, wait ~3s after wait_for_url('**/login') to let the fire-and-forget /api/auth/logout POST + AuthContext.logout's async clearStoredAuth finish (see integration issue above). Session revocation e2e is best verified by curl (captured token before Sign Out) → GET /api/auth/me → expect 401 with body {'detail':'Session revoked. Please login again.'}. The silent-refresh smoke test IS SUPPOSED to produce ~10 401 responses in the network tab on that one reload — those are the initial requests with the garbage access token that then get replayed successfully after the interceptor refresh. Don't flag those.
rca of the issue: N/A — no bugs found that block the security fix. The one race noted in `handleLogout` is a code-hygiene follow-up, not a functional regression: the server-side session-revocation table is authoritative (any leaked stale token is dead the instant /api/auth/logout hits Mongo), so even in the worst-case race window an attacker who reads localStorage in that split-second still gets a token that is already revoked.
